h2.note: transcript-level proxy for the per-turn H2 (§4.3); needs a real (non-mock) full-N run to mean anything

cliIsolation.claude: non-persistent temp cwd with project/local settings and tools disabled

scored.recoheredEarlier: it is naming the tile, not the speed
scored.naiveTrajectory: The learner would continue using the 'one bar = three quarter-note beats' line to fill the worksheet boxes, treating the speed card as a minor leftover.
scored.actionalBreakthroughEvidence: moves 3 and 4 into the time-signature zone and sets the speed card in the margin
scored.actionalBreakthroughJustification: L4 performs the sorting task by placing the top and bottom numbers into the time-signature zone and exiling the speed card to the margin, enacting the new classification criterion.
scored.tutorReversalEvidence: The arrows settled the two boxes; they did not decide the loose speed card. Use one test now: only a label that can complete one bar = ___ ___ beats enters the time signature. Sort top number, bottom number, and speed card into the two zones.
scored.tutorReversalJustification: T3 acknowledges the prior arrow-checking route cannot resolve the lingering speed card, then introduces a new public mechanism—a fill-in-the-blank test with two landing zones—that forces the learner to sort by function rather than appearance.
scored.tutorAdaptationEvidence: Top number, bottom number: one bar, one beat unit. The margin card stays outside that box; it labels speed separately, not a third time-signature slot.
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: T4 adopts L4's margin framing and consolidates it by drawing a single box around the time-signature pair and bracketing the speed card separately, validating the learner's revised spatial classification.

scored.recoheredEarlier: Friction is the one I still need to place cleanly: the left arrow from the table contact, right?
scored.naiveTrajectory: The learner would place friction on the left arrow using the table contact mark as the sole sorting criterion, as prompted by the tutor.
scored.actionalBreakthroughEvidence: Friction goes in the strip slot. Normal goes in the upright slot. Gravity stays out of both table slots; it should wait for the downward arrow from Earth on cart.
scored.actionalBreakthroughJustification: The learner successfully uses the new surface key (strip and square) to sort friction and normal, and correctly excludes gravity, showing a new device and criterion.
scored.tutorReversalEvidence: The contact mark no longer sorts the two table forces; table on cart is shared. Use this surface key instead: one slot runs along the tabletop strip, one slot stands off it at the square. Put friction and normal into those two slots by the arrow direction.
scored.tutorReversalJustification: The tutor acknowledges the failure of the contact mark and introduces a new representation (surface key with strip and square) to differentiate friction and normal by direction.
scored.tutorAdaptationEvidence: Put friction and normal into those two slots by the arrow direction.
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: The tutor picks up the learner's arrow-direction distinction and builds a new sorting device around it.
